text.title: K-factor Formula and Growth Projection
text.summary: Viral coefficient definition, interpretation thresholds, user growth projection, and viral cycle time.

rule.statement: Compute K = i × c, where i = total invites / active referrers and c = referral signups / total invites sent. Track both factors separately so you know which to optimize.
rule.rationale: K conflates two independent levers. If K drops, diagnosing whether i or c fell determines the correct fix (UX change for i, landing page for c).

example.code: Active referrers: 850 Invites sent: 4,200 → i = 4,200 / 850 = 4.94 Referral signups: 630 → c = 630 / 4,200 = 0.15 K = 4.94 × 0.15 = 0.74 (near-viral, strong amplifier)

list.item: K < 0.5: weak virality — supplement only, not a growth strategy
K = 0.5-1.0: good amplifier — each cohort grows 50-100% via referrals
K = 1.0: break-even — each user replaces themselves
K > 1.0: viral — exponential growth without ad spend
K > 2.0: highly viral — rare; Slack, WhatsApp, early Wordle territory

rule.statement: For B2B SaaS target K=0.1-0.3 baseline, K=0.5+ as "great". For consumer apps K=0.2-0.5 baseline, K=1.0+ as "great". Do not benchmark against outliers.
rule.rationale: Most benchmarks are survivorship-biased toward exceptional cases. Realistic targets by product type avoid chasing K > 1 at the expense of other channels.

rule.statement: Optimize viral cycle time (days from signup to successful referral conversion) as aggressively as K — halving cycle time approximately equals doubling K in compounding terms.
rule.rationale: K amplifies per cycle, not per day. A K=0.6 product with a 3-day cycle outgrows a K=0.8 product with a 14-day cycle over 60 days.
